IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles a concrete evaluation bottleneck in creative language generation by proposing a comprehensive manual annotation protocol and a fully automated pipeline (rhyme density + maximum similarity regression) that reproduces prior semi-automatic results and removes manual repetition penalties—contributions that are immediately useful for rap-lyric ghostwriting research. The provision of a 13-artist lyric corpus with stylistic annotations and the empirical comparison showing LSTM models generalize better than n-gram baselines strengthen its practical value. However, domain specificity (rap lyrics), acknowledged limits of the automated metrics (cannot assess vocabulary/tone/themes), and heuristic preprocessing reduce its potential to be transformative across broader NLP tasks.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear pipeline from methods to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (manual evaluation methodology, automated penalty for repetition, and an automated evaluation pipeline) are stated up front. Notation and evaluation metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, Match%) are presented and understandable, and examples/metrics seem informative. Presentation is generally adequate and related work coverage is thorough, but there are minor clarity gaps: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and preprocessing heuristics/dataset cleaning are acknowledged as imperfect, which reduces reproducibility and leaves some methodological details underspecified.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares the proposed LSTM ghostwriter against a systematic set of n-gram baselines and also replicates and improves a prior semi-automatic evaluation, demonstrating the automated pipeline reproduces earlier results—so comparisons to prior methodology are fair and acknowledged. However, comparisons omit more recent neural or Transformer-based baselines and important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are not reported, which limits assessment of full fairness and completeness despite thorough related-work discussion.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by experiments: per-artist LSTM models are compared to n-gram baselines using both manual annotations (fluency, coherence, style matching) and automated measures (rhyme density, max-similarity with repetition penalty), and the automated pipeline is reported to replicate prior semi-automatic results. There are no mathematical proofs to check. However, reproducibility and completeness are limited (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), the corpus preprocessing may leave non-verse text, and statistical testing/details of validation are not described in the summary—issues that are important but do not fundamentally undermine the experimental conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs useful comparisons (per-artist LSTM vs n-gram baselines across 13 artists) and validates a fully automated evaluation pipeline that replicates prior semi-automatic results, and it discusses limitations (evaluation blind spots, heuristic preprocessing, LSTM vocabulary issues). Related work coverage is reported as thorough. However, important reproducibility details are missing (code not mentioned, hyperparameters not reported), and the summary does not indicate systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses of the proposed automatic penalty/regression or model variants, leaving some experimental gaps.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper offers a focused, domain-specific contribution by formalizing a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol for rap verse generation and by proposing an automated pipeline (rhyme density + maximum similarity regression with an automatic repetition penalty) that replicates prior semi-automatic results. These steps are useful and represent a measurable improvement over prior semi-automatic evaluation, and the provision of a 13-artist annotated corpus is a practical dataset contribution. However, the modeling (per-artist LSTM vs n-gram baselines) and the metrics used are largely incremental applications of existing techniques rather than fundamentally new methods, so the overall novelty is moderate.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP topic—creative language generation and evaluation—by proposing a manual evaluation methodology and a fully automated pipeline (rhyme density + max similarity regression) for rap-verse generation, which is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. It uses standards expected at this venue (manual annotations for fluency/coherence, verse-level style matching, and comparisons to n-gram baselines) and evaluates per-artist LSTM models on a collected corpus of 13 artists from OHHLA, situating the work appropriately within the generation and evaluation literature. Although domain-specific, the evaluation contributions and reproducible experimental setup (automated penalty for repetition, rhyme-based metrics) are of broad interest to the conference community.

main_claims: A comprehensive manual evaluation methodology for generated rap verses assessing fluency, coherence, and style matching is presented.
An improvement to a previously semi-automatic evaluation method is introduced that automatically penalizes repetitive text, removing the need for manual intervention.

A fully automated evaluation pipeline (using rhyme density and maximum similarity regression) is shown to replicate prior semi-automatic results and is used to compare an LSTM-based ghostwriter with an n-gram baseline, with the LSTM generalizing better across artists.
A corpus of lyrics collected from OHHLA.com for (reported as) 13 rap artists is provided and manually annotated for stylistic similarity to enable evaluation.
_summary.methodology: Train per-artist LSTM RNN models (generating verses every fixed number of iterations) and evaluate generated verses with a combination of manual annotations (line-level fluency and coherence; verse-level style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density and maximum similarity regression), including an automatic penalty for repetitive text.
datasets: The Original Hip-Hop (Rap) Lyrics Archive - OHHLA.com (songs collected from OHHLA)
Corpus of lyrics for 13 rap artists (artists explicitly listed in the paper include Eminem, Jay Z, Tupac, Aesop Rock, GZA, Sage Francis, DMX, Drake, Fabolous, Notorious B.I.G., Lil’ Wayne)
metrics: Rhyme density (number of rhymed syllables / total syllables)
MatchA% (joint annotator correctness when they agree)
Line-level fluency and coherence annotation scores
limitations: Automated evaluation cannot assess certain aspects such as vocabulary, tone, and themes of an artist.
Preprocessing was heuristic and the dataset may still contain non-verse text (e.g., dialogue or chorus lines).
The LSTM model has difficulty integrating new vocabulary, making it hard to achieve desirable similarity scores.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - creative language generation (rap lyric ghostwriting)
